items.title: Why are young men turning to steroids? | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Bigorexia, or muscle dysmorphia, is a growing concern among young men. No matter how much muscle they build, many never see themselves as big enough.  

As SARMs and anabolic steroids become easier to find online — often marketed like ordinary supplements and promoted across social media — they've become increasingly visible to young men. More are taking serious risks in pursuit of the "ideal" physique, and some have paid for it with their health. 

One statistic is particularly striking: steroid dependence rates are estimated to be higher than heroin, cocaine, or alcohol.

items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>On June 6, 1944, the elite Second SS Panzer Division Das Reich was ordered to the Normandy front. As they marched across France, they left a horrific trail of massacres, including the complete destruction of Oradour-sur-Glane. Discover the dark history of Hitler's most brutal division and the controversial post-war trials that followed their escape from justice.

items.title: AI, Corporate Responsibility & Democratic Legitimacy: Extended Q&A • Joanna Bryson • GOTO 2025
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This Q&A session was recorded at GOTO Copenhagen 2025. #GOTOcon #GOTOcph
https://gotocph.com

Joanna Bryson - Professor of Ethics and Technology, Responsible Data Science for Human Development @j2bryson 

RESOURCES
https://bsky.app/profile/j2bryson.bsky.social
https://mastodon.social/@j2bryson
https://twitter.com/j2bryson
https://www.linkedin.com/in/bryson
https://joanna-bryson.blogspot.com

Links
https://digital-markets-act.ec.europa.eu/index_en

DESCRIPTION
Joanna Bryson challenges many popular assumptions about AI and AGI. She argues that current generative AI systems are not autonomous intelligences but powerful tools that compress and reproduce human cultural knowledge at scale. While these systems can appear intelligent, they remain dependent on human-created data, corporate incentives, and carefully engineered guardrails. Bryson cautions against anthropomorphizing AI and emphasizes that many concerns about AI stem from misunderstandings about how these systems actually work.

The discussion ultimately centers on responsibility and governance. Bryson argues that transparency, accountability, and democratic oversight matter far more than debates about machine consciousness. Whether discussing AI companions, recommendation algorithms, open-source models, regulation, or AI-assisted software development, her conclusion remains consistent: technology is not destiny. Engineers, companies, policymakers, and citizens all have agency in shaping how AI is deployed, regulated, and integrated into society. [...]

items.title: The Milwaukee Cannibal - "My Friend Dahmer" | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>The graphic novel "My Friend Dahmer” recounts the early years of a serial killer in the US. This deeply disturbing book by Derf Backderf, a school friend of Dahmer’s, delves into the depths of human madness.

Jeffrey Dahmer, one of the most feared serial killers in US history, was arrested in 1991. He had killed 17 men, most of them young, Black, and gay. When he learned of the crimes, Derf Backderf suddenly found himself confronted with his own past: at secondary school and sixth form, he had been on friendly terms with Dahmer, who was already considered an outsider at the time. To process his shock and make sense of it all, Backderf decided to write a graphic novel — a project spanning almost 20 years. "My Friend Dahmer” became one of the most significant works in the genre.

Drawing on previously unpublished archive footage and testimonies from Backderf’s friends, the documentary offers a deep insight into the long and sometimes painful process that went into creating this extraordinary book. It shows just how difficult it is to tell the story of a troubled teenager who appears to be like so many of his contemporaries, but who has a dark secret. How to maintain the necessary distance without succumbing to leniency or developing empathy for the future perpetrator? And how to confront one’s own past without harboring feelings of guilt? A difficult journey, that ultimately made Backderf one of the most famous contemporary comic artists in the US.

items.title: How H.H. Holmes Became America’s First Serial Killer
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Long before he was hanged in 1896 for murdering his business partner, Herman Webster Mudgett, better known as Dr. Henry Howard Holmes, had built a reputation for fraud, deception, and violence. He also built an infamous "Murder Castle" in Chicago, a labyrinthine building allegedly designed to lure and kill travelers attending the 1893 World's Fair. Historians have confirmed at least nine victims, but many believe the true death toll may have been far, far higher.

On this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift we separate the historical record from the sensational rumors surrounding H. H. Holmes, exploring the crimes, scams, and enduring mysteries that turned him into one of history's most notorious figures.

Do you believe the legends about H.H. Holmes and his"Murder Castle"? Let us know in the comments!

items.title: Does sports betting need guardrails?
items.author: Vox
items.content:
<article>The 2026 World Cup became the biggest betting event in history, but addiction and public health experts are sounding the alarm bells on the consequences of this rampant rise in sports gambling.

Since the Supreme Court removed the federal ban on sports betting in 2018, and with the rise of prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket, addiction experts have seen a measurable increase in young people seeking gambling addiction treatment. A 2026 Federal Reserve report found that states that legalized sports betting are seeing lower credit scores and increased auto delinquencies, especially among those under 40.

Is it time to add guardrails to the US sports betting industry as advertising, technology, and social media converge into a 24/7 cycle of gambling exposure?

If you or a loved one are experiencing distress from gambling, you can call 1-800-MY-RESET, the national gambling helpline that gets you connected to a counselor or therapist in your area.

items.title: When Cable TV Came to a Buddhist Kingdom (full documentary) | FRONTLINE/World
items.author: FRONTLINE PBS | Official
items.content:
<article>An exploration of the impact of television on a remote Buddhist kingdom in the Himalayas. (Aired 2002)

This journalism is made possible by viewers like you. Donate to FRONTLINE now: https://bit.ly/47DFzCb

And support your local PBS station here: https://www.pbs.org/donate

In 1999, Bhutan, a Buddhist kingdom that had remained isolated in the Himalayan mountains for centuries, became the last country in the world to legalize TV.

In the years that followed, Bhutan opened itself to what some critics called “an electronic invasion” — cable television. In this FRONTLINE/World short documentary that aired in 2002, reporters Alexis Bloom and Tshewang Dendup explored the differing reactions to the advent of cable TV in the Buddhist kingdom, including the perspective of a co-owner of a cable service company, who said, “We always have to move forward.” 

“Bhutan: The Last Place” was produced by Alexis Bloom, who was also the videographer. The co-producer and videographer was Tshewang Dendup. The founder and executive producer of FRONTLINE was David Fanning. 

We are making episodes from FRONTLINE/World’s archive newly available here on YouTube and on pbs.org as part of an effort to make our past reporting widely accessible.

items.title: Demystifying Spooky Action At A Distance, with Sean Hodgman
items.author: StarTalk
items.content:
<article>Boldly Go One Step Closer To Where It All Began. Strange New Worlds S4 Streaming July 23 on Paramount+. https://www.paramountplus.com/shows/star-trek-strange-new-worlds/

T-Mobile 5G Home Internet. https://T-Mobile.com/HomeInternet

How do you get entangled particles? Neil deGrasse Tyson and comic co-host Chuck Nice unpack the experimental side of entanglement, superposition, and the quantum underpinnings of our universe with experimental physicist, Sean Hodgman.

How do you entangle particles? Can you entangle larger things, like atoms? Sean describes his job cooling helium atoms to a millionth of a degree above absolute zero to form a Bose-Einstein condensate. Learn about momentum entanglement and why entangling atoms rather than photons matters. Could entangling atoms open the window to quantum gravity?

What is superposition, really? Sean walks Chuck through quantum superpositions, Einstein’s criticisms of “spooky action at a distance,”and Bell’s Theorem. How do you actually entangle a particle, and how do you know it worked? What are the different subtypes of entanglement beyond momentum? Has studying entanglement changed how physicists understand reality? Could you ever use entanglement for faster-than-light communication? Why does the no-communication theorem say no, while quantum encryption says there's still something useful here?

The episode closes on the biggest questions: is there an upper size limit to entanglement? Could spacetime itself emerge from a network of entangled particles? Would we notice if entanglement were switched off and could quantum effects be quietly underpinning DNA stability, bird navigation, and photosynthesis?

items.title: The Berlin rental rip-off: The housing crisis boom on tiny rooms | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Just under 6 square meters for 600 euros a month. People moving to Berlin often find only overpriced, furnished short-term rooms available. It's all due to the city's housing shortage: Germany has rent cap laws, but they don't apply to temporary, furnished accommodation.

Some landlords even take it a step further. With drywall partitions, they're dividing existing apartments into multiple tiny rooms. What was once a four-bedroom apartment now has eight people crammed into it. It's often younger people moving to Berlin who face the worst of it. Now, the boroughs of Neukölln and Pankow are tightening regulations on room rentals and expanding neighborhood protection measures to crack down on the business model. Along with tenant activists, newcomers are pushing back against high rents and sketchy rental practices. The fight is on for affordable housing and the chance to build a future in Berlin.

#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #berlin #housingcrisis

items.title: The mystery of the giant kelp forest - Luka Seamus Wright and Salomé Buglass
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Discover the ocean’s hidden kelp forests, and explore how these light-dependent organisms evolved to survive at dark depths.

--

Like any other plant, kelps require light to grow. Yet kelps found nearly 90 meters below the ocean's surface, where sometimes only 0.1% of sunlight can reach, grow as large as, and often even larger than, their shallow counterparts. So how have these large, light-dependent organisms adapted to thrive at such depths? Luka Seamus Wright and Salomé Buglass explore the ocean's hidden forests.

items.title: The truth about gut health
items.author: Vox
items.content:
<article>Worried about the spread of the diarrhea-causing illness cyclosporiasis? A healthier gut might better prepare your body to deal with it. With all the noise of wellness experts and coaches, we decided to go directly to doctors and researchers who have been studying gut health and the human microbiome for decades. 

Gut health is everywhere right now. The hashtag #GutTok has over 6 billion views on TikTok, the global digestive health product market is worth over $60 billion, and viral trends that promise to “hack your gut” through miracle products, cleanses, and restrictive diets are all the rage. But what does gut health really mean, and what can science tell us about how to make sure our gut is in good shape?

With so many voices chiming in on gut health, pushing everything from probiotic foods and supplements to wellness shots and coffee enemas, it can be hard to tell what information is based on real research and what might just be a marketing gimmick or social media clickbait. Vox producer Benjamin Stephen spoke to doctors and visited Cedars-Sinai in Los Angeles to bust some gut health myths for science-backed advice. 

Read more about gut health and gut microbiome research:

Vox editor Katherine Courage’s book, Cultured: How Ancient Foods Can Feed Our Microbiome: https://katherinecourage.com/book/ 

Cedars-Sinai’s Human Microbiome Research Institute: https://www.cedars-sinai.edu/health-sciences-university/research/departments-institutes/medicine/human-microbiome.html 

The MAST program at Cedars-Sinai: https://csmast.com/

Trisha Pasricha’s book, You’ve Been Pooping All Wrong: https://www.trishapasricha.com/youvebeenpoopingallwrong 

Vox’s explainer video on probiotics in yogurt: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sYj09lx15ik

ABSTRACT
WebAssembly started as a technology tailored to web browsers and is becoming popular as a server-side technology as well. The next step is for Wasm to become a powerful tool for cloud-native applications. When combined with Kubernetes, WebAssembly can revolutionize application deployment, security, and resource efficiency in ways traditional containers cannot.

This talk explores why and how to leverage WebAssembly within Kubernetes environments to create scalable, high-performance, and secure applications. [...]
